section.title: The Divisions of the Levites
section.sectionRationale: With Solomon now installed as king, David turns to organizing the Levites for the settled worship of the temple age. He counts them, sorts them by their ancestral clans, Gershon, Kohath, and Merari, and then revises their duties: since the ark will no longer travel and God has given Israel rest, the Levites no longer carry the tabernacle but instead serve the courts, the sacred vessels, the showbread, and the daily praise of the LORD. For the exiles reorganizing their own worship after return, this chapter is the blueprint of an ordered house, where every family and every task has its appointed place.
section.thesis: As Israel moves from a mobile tabernacle to a settled temple, David reorganizes the Levites by clan and by task, showing that when God gives His people rest, worship does not grow careless but is ordered all the more carefully, every family named, every duty assigned, a pattern the post-exilic community will re-implement and every believer since has echoed in finding their own appointed place to serve God's house.
section.frameNote: Five beats: Solomon installed and the Levites numbered, verses 1 to 5; the Gershonites, verses 6 to 11; the Kohathites, verses 12 to 20; the Merarites, verses 21 to 23; and the Levites' duties revised for the age of rest, verses 24 to 32.

verses.text: When David was old and full of years, he installed his son Solomon as king over Israel.
Then he gathered all the leaders of Israel, as well as the priests and Levites.
The Levites thirty years of age or older were counted, and the total number of men was 38,000.
"Of these," said David, "24,000 are to oversee the {{avodah|work}} of the house of the LORD, 6,000 are to be officers and judges,
4,000 are to be gatekeepers, and 4,000 are to praise the LORD with the instruments I have made for giving praise."
Then David divided the Levites into {{machlokah|divisions}} according to the sons of Levi: Gershon, Kohath, and Merari.
The Gershonites: Ladan and Shimei.
The sons of Ladan: Jehiel the first, Zetham, and Joel, three in all.
The sons of Shimei: Shelomoth, Haziel, and Haran, three in all. These were the heads of the families of Ladan.
And the sons of Shimei: Jahath, Zina, Jeush, and Beriah. These were the sons of Shimei, four in all.
Jahath was the first and Zizah was the second; but Jeush and Beriah did not have many sons, so they were counted as one family and received a single assignment.
The sons of Kohath: Amram, Izhar, Hebron, and Uzziel, four in all.

The sons of Amram: Aaron and Moses. Aaron and his descendants were set apart forever to consecrate the most holy things, to burn incense before the LORD, to minister before Him, and to pronounce blessings in His name forever.
As for Moses the man of God, his sons were named among the tribe of Levi.
The sons of Moses: Gershom and Eliezer.
The descendants of Gershom: Shebuel was the first.
The descendants of Eliezer: Rehabiah was the first. Eliezer did not have any other sons, but the sons of Rehabiah were very numerous.
The sons of Izhar: Shelomith was the first.
The sons of Hebron: Jeriah was the first, Amariah the second, Jahaziel the third, and Jekameam the fourth.
The sons of Uzziel: Micah was the first and Isshiah the second.
The sons of Merari: Mahli and Mushi. The sons of Mahli: Eleazar and Kish.
Eleazar died without having any sons; he had only daughters. Their cousins, the sons of Kish, married them.
The sons of Mushi: Mahli, Eder, and Jeremoth, three in all.

units.label: Levite duties revised for the age of rest

verses.text: These were the descendants of Levi by their families, the heads of families, registered individually by name, those twenty years of age or older who worked in the service of the house of the LORD.
For David had said, "The LORD, the God of Israel, has given {{menuchah|rest}} to His people and has come to dwell in Jerusalem forever.
So now the Levites no longer need to {{nasa|carry}} the tabernacle or any of the articles for its service."
For according to the final instructions of David, the Levites twenty years of age or older were counted,

but their duty was to assist the descendants of Aaron with the service of the house of the LORD, being responsible for the courts and chambers, the purification of all the holy things, and the work of the service of the house of God,
as well as for the rows of the showbread, the fine flour for the grain offering, the wafers of unleavened bread, the baking, the mixing, and all measurements of quantity and size.
They were also to stand every morning to give thanks and praise to the LORD, and likewise in the evening.
Whenever burnt offerings were presented to the LORD on the Sabbaths, New Moons, and appointed feasts, they were to serve regularly before the LORD in the numbers prescribed for them.
So the Levites were to carry out the responsibilities for the Tent of Meeting and the Holy Place, and, under their brothers the descendants of Aaron, the service of the house of the LORD.

avodah.gloss: Work, service, labor, the same root behind the word for a servant or worker. Twenty four thousand Levites are appointed to oversee the avodah of the house of the LORD, a term that dignifies the daily, often unglamorous labor of temple maintenance as genuine service to God.

machlokah.gloss: A division, course, or assigned group. David divides the Levites into machlokah according to their ancestral clans and further by specific tasks, gatekeeping, music, and administration, so that every Levite has a clear, named place within the ordered worship of God's house.

nasa.gloss: To lift, carry, or bear. For centuries the Levites had to nasa, carry, the tabernacle and its furnishings on their shoulders through the wilderness and the conquest; now that God has given Israel rest and come to dwell in Jerusalem, that carrying duty ends, and their labor shifts to the settled service of a permanent house.

menuchah.gloss: Rest, settledness. David explains that the LORD has given menuchah to His people, the very reason the Levites' ancient carrying duty is retired in favor of ongoing service within a permanent temple, echoing the same rest promised to Solomon's reign in the previous chapter.

basic.context: With Solomon now installed as king, aged David turns his attention to organizing the Levites for the settled worship life of the coming temple. He gathers the leaders, priests, and Levites, counts all the Levites thirty years and older, 38,000 in total, and assigns them to four categories: 24,000 to oversee the temple's work, 6,000 as officers and judges, 4,000 as gatekeepers, and 4,000 as musicians. He then organizes them by their three ancestral clans, Gershon, Kohath, and Merari, naming families down to individual sons, noting along the way that Aaron's descendants alone are set apart forever for the most holy priestly tasks, and that when a family lacked sons, daughters married within the clan to keep the inheritance intact. The chapter closes with a striking change: because God has given Israel rest and come to dwell permanently in Jerusalem, the Levites no longer need to carry the tabernacle on their shoulders. Instead, under David's final instructions, they are counted from twenty years old and assigned to assist the priests with the temple courts, the holy vessels, the showbread, and continual morning and evening praise. Key questions: why organize by both clan and specific task? Why does the minimum service age change from thirty to twenty? And what does it mean that rest changes, rather than ends, the Levites' service?
basic.hermeneutics: This is historical narrative and administrative record, describing a specific reorganization David carried out near the end of his life. It describes what David instituted; it is not a universal command binding every future generation to these exact numbers or age thresholds, since even within the Old Testament the qualifying age for Levitical service shifts here from the thirty of Numbers 4:3 to the twenty recorded as David's 'final instructions.' What endures is the underlying principle: God's people organize and dedicate themselves fully to His worship, with every person given a clear and honored place to serve.
basic.originalLanguages: David appoints Levites to the avodah, the work or service, of the house of the LORD, and organizes them into machlokah, divisions, by clan and task. Because the LORD has given menuchah, rest, and settled permanently in Jerusalem, the Levites no longer need to nasa, carry, the tabernacle, their ancient wilderness duty giving way to a settled ministry of praise and service in a fixed house.
basic.typology: The shift from carrying a mobile tabernacle to serving a settled temple pictures the movement from Israel's wilderness pilgrimage to promised rest, anticipating the greater rest Christ gives His people, Hebrews 4:9-10, and the settled, permanent dwelling of God among His people finally realized in Christ and consummated in the new creation, Revelation 21:3.
basic.christ: Aaron's descendants are set apart forever to minister and pronounce blessing in God's name, a priesthood that Hebrews shows finds its true fulfillment in Christ, our great High Priest whose priesthood, unlike Aaron's, never ends, Hebrews 7:23-25. The Levites' shift from carrying burdens to settled service in a permanent house anticipates the rest Christ gives, under whose finished work believers no longer strive to earn God's favor but serve joyfully from a place of rest already secured.
basic.god: God is a God of order, assigning specific families and individuals specific tasks in His house, showing that ordinary, daily service, courts, showbread, praise, matters as much to Him as dramatic leadership. God is also the giver of rest, the one who settles permanently among His people in Jerusalem, changing the very shape of how His servants serve Him.
basic.crossReferences: Numbers 4:1-3 sets the original minimum age of thirty for Levitical service, the standard David revises here to twenty. Ezra 2:61-63 shows the post-exilic community needing exactly this kind of preserved genealogical record to re-establish who could serve as priest and Levite. 1 Peter 2:9 calls the whole church a royal priesthood. Hebrews 7:23-25 presents Christ as the permanent, unending High Priest these ordered courses anticipated.
basic.oneStory: As God gives His people rest and settles permanently among them, their worship becomes not less careful but more ordered, every family and task given its place. This pattern of ordered, wholehearted service anticipates the true and lasting rest and priesthood found in Christ, in whom every believer, like these Levites, is given a place and a task in serving God's house.

context.literary: The chapter opens a long administrative section, chapters 23 to 27, that organizes Levites, priests, musicians, gatekeepers, and civil and military officers before David's final charge to Solomon in chapter 28. It moves from summary numbers, verses 1 to 6, to detailed genealogical listing by clan, verses 7 to 23, then to a theological explanation of why duties changed, verses 24 to 32, a pattern of summary, detail, and rationale repeated through the following chapters.
context.historical: In the wilderness and throughout the conquest and judges period, the Levites' primary labor, especially the Kohathites, was the physical transport of the tabernacle and its sacred furnishings, a demanding task requiring full physical strength from age thirty, Numbers 4:1-3. Once the ark rested permanently in Jerusalem and a fixed temple was being planned, that heavy transport duty became obsolete, freeing Levites for expanded administrative, musical, and liturgical roles, which explains David's lowering of the service age to twenty, bringing more men into a wider range of duties.
context.canonical: This detailed genealogical ordering mattered enormously to the post-exilic community the Chronicler wrote for; Ezra 2:61-63 and Nehemiah 7:63-65 show returning priests who could not prove their genealogy being excluded from priestly service until the matter could be settled. The careful family records of 1 Chronicles 23 gave the returning exiles exactly the kind of documented lineage they needed to re-establish faithful temple service.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To record how David organized the Levites by clan and by revised, settled-era duties in preparation for the temple, providing the post-exilic community with an authoritative genealogical and administrative pattern for reconstituting ordered worship after the exile.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes a specific historical reorganization, including exact numbers and a specific age of qualification for service; it is not intended as a rigid, universally binding blueprint requiring identical numbers or structures in every era of the church. What is prescriptive and abiding is the underlying principle that God's people organize themselves for wholehearted, orderly service, with every person given a clear and valued role.

debates.question: Why does David lower the minimum age for Levitical service from thirty, as in Numbers 4:3, to twenty here?

views.label: A legitimate, divinely sanctioned adaptation to changed circumstances
views.case: Verses 25-26 give David's own explicit rationale: since God has given Israel rest and the ark no longer needs to be carried, the physically demanding wilderness transport duties that required mature strength from age thirty are retired, freeing a wider range of administrative, musical, and liturgical service that younger men, from twenty, could rightly perform; this is presented as David's authoritative 'final instructions,' verse 27, not an accidental discrepancy.

views.label: Evidence of two source traditions preserved side by side
views.case: Some scholars note the text itself records two ages within the same chapter, thirty in verse 3 and twenty in verses 24 and 27, suggesting the Chronicler may be preserving two originally separate lists or stages of David's own administrative history without fully harmonizing them into one seamless account, a common feature of ancient administrative records compiled from more than one source.

debates.bearing: Either way, the change is presented as intentional and tied directly to Israel's new, settled circumstances rather than as a careless error, and it teaches that worship structures can rightly be reorganized under godly leadership as circumstances change, provided the underlying devotion to God's house remains constant.

debates.question: Why does the Chronicler record such extensive detail, individual names, birth order, and family sizes, for what might seem a purely administrative list?

views.label: Practical necessity for the post-exilic community
views.case: Returning exiles needed exactly this kind of documented lineage to re-establish who could rightly serve as priest or Levite, as Ezra 2:61-63 shows some being excluded from priestly service for lack of provable genealogy; this chapter functioned as a kind of authoritative record the post-exilic community could appeal to.

views.label: A theological statement that every name matters to God
views.case: Beyond mere administrative record-keeping, the sheer care given to naming families with few sons, verse 11, or none at all, verse 22, shows a God who does not overlook the small or seemingly insignificant family, dignifying every faithful household with a permanent place in His book, a pattern encouraging exiles engaged in unglamorous rebuilding work that their contribution is remembered and valued by God.

debates.bearing: Both are true together: the record served a real practical, legal function for the exiles while also communicating that God values and remembers every family and every faithful servant, however small their branch of the family tree.

study.originalLanguages: David organizes the Levites' avodah, service, into machlokah, divisions, by clan and task, so that every family has a defined place. The chapter's turning point comes with menuchah, rest: because the LORD has given Israel rest and settled permanently in Jerusalem, the Levites no longer need to nasa, carry, the tabernacle on their shoulders as they once did through the wilderness, their labor shifting instead to the ongoing, settled service of a permanent house of God.

typology.type: The Levites' shift from carrying a mobile tabernacle to serving a settled temple
typology.fulfillment: The rest Christ gives His people, ending the wilderness striving of self-effort for settled confidence in His finished work, Hebrews 4:9-10.

typology.type: Aaron's descendants set apart forever to minister and bless in God's name
typology.fulfillment: Christ, our great High Priest, whose priesthood, unlike Aaron's, continues forever, Hebrews 7:23-25.

typology.type: Every Levite family given a specific, named place and task
typology.fulfillment: Every believer given spiritual gifts and a place to serve within the body of Christ, 1 Corinthians 12:4-7, 1 Peter 4:10.

study.christ: Aaron and his descendants are set apart forever in this chapter to consecrate the holy things, minister before the LORD, and pronounce blessing in His name, a priesthood the New Testament shows was always pointing beyond itself; Christ is our true and permanent High Priest, holding His priesthood permanently because He continues forever, able to save completely those who draw near to God through Him, Hebrews 7:23-25. The Levites' transition from the exhausting labor of carrying the tabernacle to the settled service of a permanent temple, because God has given Israel rest, pictures the greater rest Christ secures for His people, who no longer strive to earn God's favor but serve joyfully from a place of rest already won, Hebrews 4:9-10, Matthew 11:28-30.
study.god: God is a God of order who cares about the details of how His people organize their worship, naming individual families and assigning specific, honored tasks so that no faithful servant is overlooked. God is also the giver of rest, and it is precisely because He has given Israel rest and come to dwell permanently among them that the shape of faithful service changes, showing that true rest in God does not lead to careless worship but to worship reorganized around gratitude for what He has already given.

crossReferences.note: Sets the original minimum age of thirty for Levitical service in the wilderness tabernacle, the standard David revises here to twenty for settled temple service.
Returning exiles who could not prove their priestly genealogy were excluded from service until the matter could be resolved, showing exactly why such careful family records mattered.
Christ's permanent, unending priesthood, of which the Aaronic priesthood set apart in this chapter was always a foreshadowing.
The Sabbath rest that remains for God's people, of which the settled rest that ended the Levites' carrying duty is a foretaste.

study.oneStory: As God gives Israel rest and settles permanently among them in Jerusalem, David reorganizes the Levites, every clan and family named, every task assigned, so that ordered, wholehearted worship becomes the shape of life in the age of rest. That pattern anticipates the greater rest and the greater, permanent priesthood found in Christ, in whom every believer, like these Levites, is given a place and a task in serving the living house of God.

quiz.q: How did David divide the 38,000 Levites counted in this chapter?

options.text: 24,000 to oversee the work of the house of the LORD, 6,000 as officers and judges, 4,000 as gatekeepers, and 4,000 to praise the LORD with instruments.
options.note: Verses 4-5 give this precise fourfold division of duties.

options.text: All 38,000 were assigned equally to gatekeeping duties alone.
options.note: The text divides the Levites into four distinct categories of service, not one single equal assignment.

options.text: Half were assigned to temple work and half to military service under Joab.
options.note: The chapter's fourfold division does not include military service under Joab; that falls to other officials described elsewhere.

options.text: Most were assigned as musicians, with smaller numbers for other tasks.
options.note: Musicians were the smallest of the four groups, 4,000, while overseeing the temple's work was by far the largest assignment, 24,000.

quiz.q: Into what three ancestral divisions did David organize the Levites by clan?

options.text: Gershon, Kohath, and Merari, the three sons of Levi.
options.note: Verse 6 names these three clans, matching the ancient threefold division of the Levites going back to Levi's own sons.

options.text: Aaron, Moses, and Eleazar.
options.note: These are individuals within the Kohathite line, not the three overarching Levitical clans.

options.text: Judah, Benjamin, and Simeon.
options.note: These are tribes of Israel outside the tribe of Levi altogether, not Levitical clan divisions.

options.text: Gershon, Kohath, and Aaron.
options.note: Gershon and Kohath are correct, but the third clan is Merari, not Aaron, who belongs within the Kohathite line as a descendant of Amram.

quiz.q: Why, according to David, did the Levites no longer need to carry the tabernacle?

options.text: Because the LORD had given Israel rest and come to dwell permanently in Jerusalem.
options.note: Verses 25-26 state this explicitly as David's own reasoning for the change.

options.text: Because the tabernacle itself had been destroyed and no longer existed.
options.note: The tabernacle was still standing at Gibeon, mentioned in the previous chapter's context; it was not destroyed.

options.text: Because the Levites had grown too few in number to carry it.
options.note: The opposite; 38,000 Levites were counted, a substantial number, and the reason given is God's rest, not a shortage of manpower.

options.text: Because Solomon had ordered the change as soon as he became king.
options.note: The change is attributed to David's own final instructions, given before Solomon's reign properly began, not a decision made by Solomon himself.

quiz.q: What special, permanent role is assigned to Aaron and his descendants in this chapter?

options.text: To consecrate the most holy things, burn incense before the LORD, minister before Him, and pronounce blessings in His name forever.
options.note: Verse 13 sets Aaron's line apart specifically and permanently for these priestly tasks.

options.text: To oversee the census of fighting men for future wars.
options.note: Numbering fighting men was David's own project in chapter 21, not a duty assigned to Aaron's descendants here.

options.text: To serve exclusively as gatekeepers for the temple courts.
options.note: Gatekeeping was a distinct Levitical assignment, not the specific priestly role given to Aaron's line in verse 13.

options.text: To lead the nation's civil government alongside the king.
options.note: Other Levites are assigned as officers and judges, verse 4, but Aaron's specific and permanent role named in verse 13 concerns holy things, incense, ministry, and blessing, not civil government.

quiz.q: What happened when a Levite family had no sons, as with Eleazar the son of Mahli?

options.text: His daughters married their cousins, the sons of Kish, keeping the family line and inheritance within the clan.
options.note: Verse 22 records this specific solution for Eleazar's family, who had only daughters.

options.text: The family line was simply removed from all future Levitical records.
options.note: The opposite: the text carefully preserves this family's continuation through the daughters' marriages to cousins.

options.text: The family's duties were transferred entirely to the tribe of Judah.
options.note: No such transfer outside the tribe of Levi is described; the solution kept the family and its assignment within the Levitical clan itself.

options.text: The daughters themselves were counted among the 38,000 Levites for service.
options.note: The census specifically counted Levite men for service; the daughters' marriages preserved the family line and inheritance rather than making them counted temple servants themselves.

questions: Every Levite family, even the smallest, was named and given a specific task. Where do you need to remember that your seemingly small or ordinary role in serving God still matters deeply to Him?
The Levites' duties changed once God gave Israel rest; carrying gave way to settled, ongoing service. Where has God given you a season of rest that should reshape, rather than end, how you serve Him?

Aaron's line was set apart forever to minister and bless in God's name, fulfilled fully in Christ our eternal High Priest. How does resting in Christ's permanent priesthood free you from needing to earn your standing before God?

When a family lacked sons, provision was still made to keep them within the covenant community. How does this shape the way you think about caring for those who feel overlooked or without an obvious place to serve?

This whole chapter is about order: names, numbers, and tasks, all carefully arranged for worship. What would it look like for you to bring the same intentional care to how you organize your own time and gifts for serving God's people?

translationNotes.note: The chapter records Levites counted from age thirty in verse 3, matching the standard set in Numbers 4:3, but then records them counted from age twenty in verses 24 and 27, explicitly called David's 'final instructions.' This is honestly presented in the hermeneutics debates above as either David's own explained adaptation to the settled temple era, verses 25-26, or the preservation of two administrative stages within the same record.
The BSB footnote notes that Ladan is a variant of Libni, the name used for this same clan in 1 Chronicles 6:17, likely reflecting an alternate family name rather than a different person.
The BSB footnote notes a Hebrew manuscript and versional variant, Zina or Zizah, for this name in verses 10 and 11, a minor spelling variation not affecting the passage's meaning.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for 1 Chronicles 23:1-32. No em dashes appeared in the source text for this chapter; the text required no dash conversions. A search of Alistair Begg, Tim Keller, John Piper, Sinclair Ferguson, and D.A. Carson's available sermon archives found no message preached specifically on this chapter, so sermon status is recorded as none.
